---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
#format: revealjs
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric}
  \usetikzlibrary{overlay-beamer-styles}
  \usepackage{booktabs}
---


```{=latex}
% TITLE SLIDES
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{11.3 - Comparing scores across tests}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{

\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```



## Comparing scores across tests

A patient scores **42** on a depression scale and **61** on an anxiety scale. Which symptom is more severe?

\pause

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.5em}
Raw scores are on \textbf{different scales} and they cannot be directly compared. We need a \emph{common metric}.
```

## Z-scores as a common metric

A **z-score** expresses a raw score in units of standard deviations from the normative mean:

$$z = \frac{X - M}{SD}$$

\pause

This allows comparison across tests with different scales and units, \textbf{assuming the normative distributions are comparable}.

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.5em}
A $z = +2.0$ on depression and $z = +1.2$ on anxiety tells us immediately that depression deviates more from the norm regardless of the original scales.
```

## From z-scores to percentiles

If the normative distribution is approximately **normal**, z-scores map directly onto **percentile ranks**:

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.6em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\toprule
\textbf{z-score} & \textbf{Percentile} & {\small \textbf{Potential Interpretation}} \\
\midrule
$-2.0$ & 2.3\% & Far below average \\
$-1.0$ & 15.9\% & Below average \\
$\phantom{-}0.0$ & 50.0\% & Average \\
$+1.0$ & 84.1\% & Above average \\
$+2.0$ & 97.7\% & Far above average \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
Percentiles provide an \textbf{intuitive, scale-free} summary: a score at the 97th percentile is high \emph{regardless} of which test produced it.
```

## Test profiles
```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
#| fig.width: 6.5
#| fig.height: 4
#| fig.dpi: 100

source("R_functions/radio_barplot.R")

radio_barplot()


```


## Limitations (1): comparing across different samples

```{=latex}
This approach rests on a critical assumption: \textbf{all norms come from equivalent populations}.
\pause

\vspace{0.5em}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Different tests are often normed on \textbf{different samples} (different countries, ages, education levels, education, recruitment criteria).
  \vspace{0.3em}
  \item A $z = +1.5$ on Test A (normed on a community sample) is \textbf{not} directly comparable to $z = +1.5$ on Test B (normed on a clinical sample).
  \vspace{0.3em}
  \item Normative samples vary in \textbf{size}: a percentile derived from $n = 80$ carries far more uncertainty than one from $n = 2000$, yet both are typically reported as point estimates with no uncertainty attached.
  \vspace{0.3em}
  \item Percentile equivalence across tests is \textbf{an assumption, not a fact}: and it is often violated silently.
\end{itemize}
```

## Limitation (2): the normality assumption

Percentiles derived from z-scores are only valid when the normative distribution is **approximately normal**.

\pause

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.4em}
Many psychopathology scales are \textbf{skewed} in community samples (most people score near zero). In such cases:

\begin{itemize}
  \item The $z \to$ percentile conversion is \textbf{inaccurate}.
  \item Empirical percentiles (directly from the normative sample) should be preferred over those derived from the normal distribution.
\end{itemize}
```

## Limitation (3): multivariate patterns?

A pattern of scores can be entirely consistent with expected _multivariate relationships_.

For example, a very high score on one scale (e.g., Social Withdrawal) may be associated with a low score on another (e.g., Extroversion).
Therefore, a simple high–low contrast is not, in itself, evidence of something problematic.

_Note: this problem holds whenever we used norms that treat each test separately_

## Limitations (4): no statistical inference

Comparing percentiles across tests provides **no statistical inference**.

\pause

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.4em}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Saying \textit{``the patient is at the 85th percentile on anxiety and at the 72nd on depression''} is a \textbf{descriptive} statement, but no test of whether the two scores are \emph{significantly} different (i.e., no $p$-value).
  \vspace{0.3em}
  \item \pause The difference between two percentiles could easily be due to \textbf{sampling variability} in the two normative samples, to \textbf{measurement error} in the two tests, or both.
  \vspace{0.3em}
  \item \pause There is no formal procedure, within a pure percentile-comparison framework, to test the null hypothesis that the patient performs \emph{equally} on both constructs.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.4em}
\pause
Methods such as those proposed by \textbf{Crawford \& Howell (1998)} or \textbf{Crawford \& Garthwaite (2006)} provide proper inferential tests for individual scores and score differences against normative data.
```

## Summary: when percentile comparison works, and when it does not

```{=latex}
\begin{itemize}
  \item[\textbf{+}] Percentiles and z-scores offer a \textbf{convenient common metric} when tests share comparable normative populations.
  \vspace{0.3em}
  \item[\textbf{--}] Results are \textbf{not interpretable} when normative samples differ in composition, size, or recruitment strategy.
  \vspace{0.3em}
  \item[\textbf{--}] The normality assumption can be violated in many psychological measures.
  \vspace{0.3em}
  \item[\textbf{--}] Percentile comparison is \textbf{purely descriptive}: it cannot answer whether two scores are statistically different from each other or from the norm.
\end{itemize}

```

## Conclusion

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.5em}
\begin{alertblock}{Bottom line}
Percentile comparison is an intuitive but statistically weak tool. For rigorous individual-level inference, formal methods that model measurement error, multivariate patterns, and normative uncertainty are required.
\end{alertblock}
```
